participants.reasoning: The extracted text matches the official criteria and demographics overall, and the reduction from 37 enrolled to 29 analyzed is explained by dropout.
participants.extracted: Thirty-seven physically healthy adults with schizophrenia were recruited from the Maryland Psychiatric Research Center residential research unit in Baltimore, Maryland. Diagnosis of schizophrenia was confirmed by two independent research psychiatrists using DSM-IV criteria based on the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-III-R and clinical history. Participants were aged 19 to 60 years, with illness duration ranging from 1 to 44 years. The cohort included 22 males and 7 females, and 8 Caucasians and 21 African Americans. All participants were on stable antipsychotic treatment before study entry, had no long-acting depot antipsychotic use, and were required to be capable of understanding study risks and to provide informed consent. After a 2-week psychotropic medication washout, 8 of the 37 enrolled participants dropped out during the study period.
participants.official:
Diagnosis: schizophrenia (DSM‐IV)
History: informed consent obtained
N = 29
Age: 19 to 60, mean age (Hal) 38, (Olan) 36 years
Sex: M 22, F 7
Setting: inpatient
Inclusion criteria: diagnosis of schizophrenia by DSM‐IV
Exclusion criteria: comorbid diseases; no further details

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention details, including the two drugs and their reported dose ranges.
interventions.extracted: Oral haloperidol and oral olanzapine were compared. After a 2-week medication washout, participants were blindly randomized to receive either haloperidol 10 mg daily for 6 days followed by a clinically determined optimal dose of 10–20 mg for 5 weeks, or olanzapine 12.5 mg daily for 6 days followed by a clinically determined optimal dose of 12.5–25 mg for 5 weeks. Similar-looking capsules were used, and doses were adjusted in blinded fashion by treating psychiatrists; anticholinergic medication was allowed as needed. The analysis combined the active-treatment groups to examine the common effects of antipsychotic drug treatment on functional connectivity.
interventions.official:
Haloperidol: dose range 10 to 20 mg/day; N = 14
Olanzapine: dose range 12.5 to 25 mg/day; N = 18

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes add specific task-performance measures and detailed PET connectivity endpoints that are not reflected in the official outcomes, which list only BPRS, extrapyramidal adverse effects, and H215O PET scanning.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure clinical symptoms using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS), including the Psychosis Subscale, assessed at baseline before restarting antipsychotic treatment, at 1 week after 6 days of treatment, and at 6 weeks after continuous treatment. It also planned to measure task performance outcomes during the sensorimotor control and auditory discrimination tasks, specifically response accuracy/error rate and reaction time, assessed at each scanning session across baseline, 1 week, and 6 weeks. In addition, the study planned to measure regional cerebral blood flow and functional connectivity among predefined regions of interest (medial frontal cortex, nucleus accumbens, and hippocampus) using H215O PET, assessed at baseline, 1 week, and 6 weeks, both during individual task conditions and in a combined all-tasks condition.
outcomes.official:
Mental state: BPRS
Adverse effects: extrapyramidal
H215O PET scanning
